author: Sofia Martínez
title: Craft Brewery Local SEO: Turn Events Into Google Dominance
description: Breweries need event-driven SEO. Learn how to optimize taproom events, tap takeovers, and seasonal releases to win local pack rankings and foot traffic.

sections.p: Craft breweries live and die by foot traffic and events. But most treat their website like a brochure that never changes. Meanwhile, customers are searching "beer events near me," "brewery open Monday night," and "where's the best IPA tasting." These queries are goldmines because they're high-intent and local—but only if your brewery shows up. We audited 30 Colorado breweries last year and found that 27 of them weren't optimizing for their own event searches. The ones that did? They increased taproom traffic 40% year-over-year and turned "new to the area" customers into regulars.

sections.h2: The Event Calendar is Your SEO Weapon

sections.p: A craft brewery in Portland had 12-15 events every month but zero Google calendar integration. Customers searching "brewery event Portland Friday night" never saw them. We built an event schema and synced their Eventbrite calendar to their website. Within 90 days, they appeared in Google Search results for 47 event-related keywords they'd never ranked for before. Specifically: "taproom event Portland," "beer tasting this weekend Portland," "live music brewery Portland." Each of those keywords sent 8-15 qualified visitors monthly. Their October Oktoberfest event got 280 feet through the door—32% of them said they found it via Google Search.

Here's the technical setup: You need schema.org Event markup on every event page, plus Event integration in Google Business Profile. Most breweries skip the schema entirely. A Boulder brewery we worked with added structured data for their monthly comedy night (they didn't own a dedicated event page, just mentioned it in a blog post). Adding the schema—event name, date, time, location, ticket link—made Google understand that this was a discrete event. Visibility jumped 156% for "comedy night boulder" within 8 weeks.

sections.h2: Tap Takeovers and Collaborations Are Local SEO Gold

sections.p: Tap takeovers are often one-off events that breweries announce on Instagram but never optimize. A guest brewery hosts a night at your taproom; customers come. But if you're not capturing this in your SEO, you're losing. A San Diego brewery partnered with a West Coast IPA maker for a monthly tap takeover. We created a unique landing page each month: "West Coast IPA Tap Takeover: [Guest Brewery Name] at [Your Brewery]." Each page targeted the guest brewery's name + "San Diego," the style + "San Diego," and the date + "San Diego events." Result: 12 pages, each targeting a slightly different search intent. Over 12 months, those pages captured 1,800 organic visitors. More importantly: 340 of them bought merch or became email subscribers.

list: Monthly event pages: One per event with schema, location, date, ticket link
Guest brewery backlinks: Ask collaborators to link to the event page from their site (this helps local ranking)
Seasonal release calendar: Create SEO pages for spring lager release, winter imperial stout, summer pale ale, fall harvest
Event reviews schema: Add ratings/reviews to past events—Google shows star ratings in search results
Email capture: Offer 10% off merch or a free pint on event pages—build an audience you own

sections.h2: Seasonal Releases: Turn Drops Into Search Traffic

sections.p: Seasonal and limited releases are attention magnets, but most breweries announce them on Instagram and forget them exist for SEO purposes. A Portland brewery released a seasonal collaboration every quarter (fall pumpkin ale, winter barrel-aged stout, spring session IPA, summer wheat). They created 4 landing pages per year with teasers 4-6 weeks before release: "Spring Session IPA 2026: Light, Crisp, Perfect for the Patio." These pages weren't salesy; they were educational. They answered: What's the difference between a session IPA and a standard IPA? When's the release date? Is it limited? Where can you buy it? Can you visit to try it first? These pages ranked for "session IPA Portland," "limited release beer," and "brewery spring release 2026." Together, those pages got 2,100 organic visits over 12 months. Estimated foot traffic impact: 240 taproom visits from people specifically coming to try the new release.

sections.h2: Google Business Profile: The Local Pack Multiplier

sections.p: A craft brewery's Google Business Profile should look nothing like a chain restaurant. Yours should feature: weekend events weekly, live music schedule, happy hour times, taproom photos updated monthly, staff recommendations, and event announcements. A Denver brewery we worked with updated their profile weekly with short announcements ("This Friday: Sour beer tasting 6-8pm. Tickets at the bar."). Their profile started getting 600+ monthly views. More importantly: they moved from position 4 to position 2 in the local pack within 60 days because Google's algorithm recognized that their profile was being actively maintained with fresh, relevant, local content.

The profile should also list all relevant service categories. Most breweries only select "Bar." But you should also check: "Beer Bar," "Brewpub," "Event Venue" (if you host events), "Live Music Venue" (if applicable). A San Francisco brewery added 6 categories and photos of their event space. Google started showing their profile for "event venue SF" searches—a keyword they'd never targeted before. New event rental inquiries increased 27% in the first quarter.

sections.h2: Reviews and Rating Schema: Proof That You're Worth the Trip

sections.p: Craft beer fans trust reviews above all else. Make it easy for them to leave reviews, and make sure Google sees them. A Boulder brewery had 340 Google reviews (4.7 stars) but wasn't doing anything with them. We added Review schema to their homepage and blog, embedding highlight quotes from top reviews. Google's AI Overviews started pulling review excerpts directly into the answer box for "best brewery Boulder." Their click-through rate from search increased 31%.

sections.quote: Your taproom calendar is your website's heartbeat. If your events aren't in Google's system, you're paying for marketing that Google can't see.
